title: The future is fermented: How Europe can succeed in the next industrial race – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Janka Oertel argues biomanufacturing — using engineered microbes and fermentation, turbocharged by AI and cheap renewables, to make food, fuel, chemicals and materials without fossil inputs — is becoming a foundational industrial layer as geopolitically consequential as fossil fuels once were; China is pursuing state-backed dominance across capital, infrastructure, energy, feedstock and AI, and Europe risks repeating its solar/battery/EV pattern of inventing the technology while ceding production and leverage to China unless it urgently scales infrastructure, cuts regulatory approval times and builds coalitions with the UK, Japan and India.

themes.theme: Biomanufacturing as the next industrial-geopolitical layer

insights.claim: Biomanufacturing — using biological processes to produce food, fuels, chemicals and materials that today rely on fossil resources — is transforming from a niche climate solution into a new production paradigm with the potential to reshape industrial competitiveness and geopolitical power much as fossil fuels did in the previous industrial age.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Frames biomanufacturing as a macro industrial-policy theme on par with semiconductors and rare earths — a long-horizon sector to track for geopolitical leverage, not just ESG allocation.
insights.locator: The microbe revolution

insights.claim: The advances driving biomanufacturing's takeoff are AI (accelerating design, prototyping and testing of engineered microorganisms) combined with falling renewable energy costs, since fermentation is energy-intensive and margins outside pharmaceuticals are thin.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Identifies the two enabling tailwinds (AI + cheap power) that determine which regions/firms can scale biomanufacturing profitably — a filter for identifying likely winners.
insights.locator: Driven by abundant and affordable renewable energy

insights.claim: The US-China Economic and Security Review Commission's 2025 report to Congress likened fermentation capacity to semiconductor fab capacity, warning China's dominance could become a strategic chokepoint extending across future food, pharmaceuticals, chemicals and energy inputs.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
entities: US-China Economic and Security Review Commission
insights.so_what: An official US government body has already flagged fermentation capacity as a chip-fab-level chokepoint risk — a strong signal this becomes a policy and export-control battleground.
insights.locator: In a 2025 report to Congress

insights.claim: If Europe fails to act with urgency, Oertel warns it risks repeating the pattern seen in solar panels, batteries and electric vehicles: pioneering the breakthrough technology while ceding production, profits and geopolitical leverage to China — this time with even greater consequences given biomanufacturing's broader reach into food, energy and materials.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: The article's central warning and call to action — the base case is European technological leadership converting into Chinese industrial dominance, as happened in three prior clean-tech waves.
insights.locator: Without greater urgency, Europe risks repeating the mistakes

insights.claim: China already accounts for more than 70% of global basic fermentation capacity and is reportedly spending €2bn-3bn annually on biotechnology R&D, after spending an average of roughly €1.4bn per year on EV R&D over 15 years to build its dominant EV industry.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Quantifies China's existing capacity lead (70%+ of basic fermentation) and its R&D spend trajectory, which is already outpacing its earlier (successful) EV R&D push in relative terms.
insights.locator: Beijing has spent an average of roughly €1.4bn

insights.claim: China's 2022 Bioeconomy Five-Year Plan set a goal of global biomanufacturing leadership by 2035, and the 2026 15th Five-Year Plan places biomanufacturing alongside AI at the heart of China's future industrial model; in July 2025 Beijing named 35 flagship biomanufacturing products to replace fossil-based chemicals or food/feed additives and selected 43 companies to build pilot plants.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Concrete, dated policy targets (2035 leadership goal; 35 products, 43 pilot companies) that investors can use as milestones to track China's execution pace.
insights.locator: The 2022 Bioeconomy Five-Year Plan

insights.claim: China's biomanufacturing advantage does not stem from a single scientific breakthrough but from integrating five mutually reinforcing pillars — patient capital, industrial infrastructure, cheap energy, feedstock access, and cost-competitive AI — into one ecosystem, per analysts Dirk van der Kley and Tom Campbell.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Gives investors a five-factor framework for scoring any country's or company's biomanufacturing competitiveness, not just capital or headline technology.
insights.locator: China’s advantage is not due to a single scientific breakthrough

insights.claim: China's industrial electricity prices are among the lowest in the world — roughly 50% of the global average — due to renewable expansion, continued nuclear and domestic coal investment, state control over pricing, and regional pricing policies designed to attract industry to specific provinces.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Cheap, state-directed power (about half the global average) is a structural cost advantage for Chinese fermentation that Europe cannot easily replicate given its post-2022 energy price shock.
insights.locator: China’s industrial electricity prices are among the lowest

insights.claim: Under Xi Jinping, China has shifted from longstanding scepticism of genetically modified organisms toward pragmatic support for domestic GMO innovation and food security, exemplified by state-owned ChemChina's 2017 takeover of European seed and agtech giant Syngenta.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: The Syngenta acquisition shows China already owns a top-tier Western seed/agtech asset, a concrete precedent for how it plans to close its feedstock-technology gap.
insights.locator: The takeover of European seed and agricultural technology giant Syngenta

insights.claim: China's leadership released a list of 16 typical application cases for AI in biomanufacturing in 2025 and is actively pushing companies to integrate AI into manufacturing; cheaper Chinese AI models are driving adoption in thin-margin sectors both domestically and abroad.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Cheap Chinese AI models becoming an input to global biomanufacturing adoption is a second-order export channel for Chinese AI beyond direct chip/model sales.
insights.locator: The Chinese leadership is prioritising the industrial use

themes.theme: Europe's strengths, gaps and chokepoint exposure

insights.claim: Europe's biosolutions sector is worth €60bn (comparable to Slovenia's GDP) and growing around 5% annually; if scaled successfully, biomanufacturing could replace a quarter of European oil-based chemicals, halve the continent's protein imports, cut greenhouse gas emissions and create 700,000 additional jobs by as soon as 2030.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Quantifies the addressable upside for Europe (700,000 jobs, 25% of oil-based chemicals displaced) if it executes — a scale reference for sizing the opportunity.
insights.locator: Europeans are at the cutting-edge of research

insights.claim: European start-ups are increasingly tempted to manufacture and scale in China because of its fermentation capacity, exposing valuable European intellectual property to a fierce competitor and risking the same know-how migration seen in batteries and other clean technologies.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: A live IP-leakage risk mechanism: European biotech founders scaling in China today create tomorrow's Chinese competitors — a specific due-diligence flag for European biotech investors.
insights.locator: As China scales its fermentation capacity

insights.claim: Europe holds twice the precision fermentation capacity of the US (479 demonstration/semi-industrial/pilot plants plus 2,362 additional biorefineries) and 47% of global protein and enzyme fermentation capacity for industrial applications, yet still faces a plant-protein deficit of nearly 19m tonnes, largely for livestock feed.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Shows Europe already has more installed fermentation infrastructure than the US — the constraint is capital/regulatory speed, not physical capacity, which changes the policy prescription.
insights.locator: Europe holds twice the precision fermentation capacity

insights.claim: Alternative proteins alone could contribute €111bn in annual gross value added to the EU economy by 2040 (comparable to Europe's entire wine sector) and build a €60bn export market comparable to current EU exports to South Korea; Europe's biofuel market already exceeded $43.7bn in 2025 and is projected to grow 9.4% annually.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Concrete revenue-pool sizing (€111bn GVA, €60bn exports, $43.7bn biofuel market growing at 9.4%/yr) for investors underwriting European biomanufacturing exposure.
insights.locator: With the right support, alternative proteins alone

insights.claim: The EU is creating regulatory-driven demand — a 29% renewable-transport-energy share or 14.5% emissions-intensity cut by 2030, sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) rising from 0.6% of EU airport fuel in 2024 to a mandated 70% by 2050, and FuelEU Maritime targeting 6% emissions cuts by 2030 and 80% by 2050 — but 69% of the feedstocks used for EU SAF production currently originate outside the bloc, with China supplying 38% and Malaysia 12%.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term risk: EU regulation is manufacturing demand for SAF/biofuels that European firms are not currently positioned to capture, with China already the largest foreign feedstock supplier — a leakage the article flags as urgent to close.
insights.locator: Today, 69% of feedstocks used for production of SAF

insights.claim: Regulatory delay is a core European weakness: novel food authorisations average 2.5 years (up to six), GMO crop approvals take up to six years versus under two in the US, and biopesticide approvals average seven to nine years — versus Denmark's EvodiaBio, which moved from lab to profitable industrial production in just three and a half years.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Quantifies the regulatory-speed gap (3-4x slower than the US in places) as the single most fixable European bottleneck, with Denmark's EvodiaBio as a proof point that fast approval is achievable within the EU.
insights.locator: Across the EU, novel food authorisations average 2.5 years

insights.claim: Shell cancelled a sustainable-aviation-fuel plant in the Netherlands in 2025, losing $600m in investment, illustrating the capital-deterrence risk that persists in European biomanufacturing despite legislative demand mandates.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: A concrete, named loss ($600m Shell write-off) evidencing that EU demand mandates alone are not de-risking capital enough to prevent project cancellations — a caution for SAF-plant investment theses in Europe.
insights.locator: Shell lost $600m in investments

insights.claim: NATO elevated biotech to a strategic priority with its first dedicated strategy in 2024 and now channels over €500m annually to 28 dual-use biotech startups via the DIANA programme, with defence applications including bio-based armour, drones, rare-earth substitutes and biosensors for chemical/biological threat detection.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Identifies a funded, growing defence-biotech channel (€500m/yr, 28 startups) as a near-term catalyst independent of civilian biomanufacturing economics.
insights.locator: NATO elevated biotech to a strategic priority

insights.claim: Germany's AMSilk is the world's first industrial-scale producer of spider-silk proteins, partnering with Airbus on lightweight aerospace composites and with Evonik for fermentation supply, and scaled its protein production with Denmark's 21st.BIO in 2024; Dutch firm Avantium has begun commercial-scale production of PEF, a fully recyclable bio-based alternative to fossil-based PET plastic.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Names specific European companies (AMSilk, Avantium) already commercializing advanced biomaterials — concrete names for investors screening European biomanufacturing leaders.
insights.locator: Germany’s AMSilk, for example, is the world’s first

insights.claim: Fermentation-produced chymosin (FPC), which replaced animal rennet in cheesemaking, became widespread in the 1990s and today accounts for 80-90% of all commercial cheese production globally — the clearest precedent that precision fermentation can fully displace an incumbent input at global scale.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: A proof-of-concept case study (80-90% global market capture) that de-risks the thesis that precision-fermentation products can achieve full-scale incumbent displacement, not just niche adoption.
insights.locator: Fermentation-produced chymosin (FPC)

insights.claim: China must feed 19% of the world's population with only 8% of its arable land; food self-sufficiency fell from 94% to 66% between 2000 and 2020, 2025 soybean imports hit a record 111.83m metric tonnes, and China could face a protein feed shortfall of 120m tonnes by 2035 — making protein security a national-security priority driving its biomanufacturing push.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Quantifies China's structural food-security deficit (120m tonne 2035 shortfall) as the demand-side driver behind its biomanufacturing investment — explains the strategic urgency behind Beijing's spending, not just industrial ambition.
insights.quote: China must keep its rice bowls firmly
insights.locator: This matters particularly for China.

insights.claim: Europeans rarely discuss protein security, having lost the memory of food poverty amid decades of agricultural abundance and subsidy, while agricultural lobbies keep pushing exports of low-value goods (Swedish oats, Spanish pork, Irish dairy/seafood, German chicken feet) to China rather than building resilience in higher-value biomanufactured protein.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: A contrarian critique that Europe's current China-facing agricultural trade (low-value commodity exports) is strategically shortsighted relative to building high-value biomanufacturing capacity.
insights.locator: Europeans, on the other hand, rarely talk about protein security

insights.claim: Sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) can be blended up to 50% with conventional jet kerosene without aircraft modification, cutting lifecycle emissions up to 80%; since air forces account for 85% of total military fuel consumption in a major conflict, the UK Ministry of Defence mandated that all aviation fuel procured from January 2025 include SAF.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Ties SAF directly to military fuel security (85% of conflict-time fuel use is aviation), turning it into a defence-procurement as well as a decarbonisation story — a UK MOD mandate is already in force.
insights.locator: In a major conflict, air forces account for 85%

insights.claim: China controls 55% of global shipbuilding output and 75% of new orders, and is already running sea trials for the world's largest methanol dual-fuel container ship (built by Nantong COSCO KHI) and building the first deep-sea bulk carriers with ethanol-powered engines, positioning Chinese shipyards to set the standards for biofuel-ready shipping and squeeze out European carriers like Maersk or Hapag-Lloyd that don't embed into Chinese supply chains.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: China's shipbuilding dominance (55% of output) combined with early biofuel-ship trials gives it first-mover standard-setting power in maritime decarbonisation — a structural risk for European shipping lines' supply-chain independence.
insights.locator: China is the world’s largest shipbuilder

insights.claim: Denmark has built one of the world's most advanced biomanufacturing ecosystems by combining strong fermentation infrastructure with supportive policy; roughly 20% of Danish land is organically farmed, and Novonesis produces microbial inoculants boosting crop yields 3-7% while cutting fertiliser use, positioning biotech as a complement to agriculture rather than a threat.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Denmark is the clearest European biomanufacturing leader (fast approvals plus integrated agri-biotech ecosystem) and the reference model other EU states should replicate.
insights.locator: By combining strong fermentation infrastructure

insights.claim: Germany is Europe's second biotech powerhouse: Sartorius, BioNTech and Merck KGaA together hold roughly 25% of EU precision fermentation capacity for low-carbon chemicals, Formo leads in fermentation-derived proteins, and Bayer and BASF are advancing biopesticides and other crop-protection agents.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Names the concentrated set of German companies (25% of EU low-carbon-chemical fermentation capacity) that are the investable core of Europe's biomanufacturing industrial base.
insights.locator: Germany is Europe’s second biotech powerhouse

insights.claim: Switzerland dominates global contract development and manufacturing (CDMO) for biological products through companies like Lonza and Roche, which own the fermentation tanks and purification infrastructure that biosolutions IP-holders rely on to manufacture at scale.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Identifies Swiss CDMOs (Lonza, Roche) as critical infrastructure gatekeepers in the European biomanufacturing value chain, distinct from the IP-holding biosolutions firms.
insights.locator: Switzerland dominates contract development

insights.claim: The Netherlands leads Europe in cultivated meat and precision fermentation via DSM-Firmenich and Avantium; France has a robust agricultural biomanufacturing base with Standing Ovation and Valley in alternative proteins and De Sangosse (€315m) in biopesticides; Italy ranks third in the EU by organic farmland, with Rovensa Next leading in biostimulants and biocontrol.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Maps the remaining national specialisations (Netherlands, France, Italy) that round out Europe's distributed biomanufacturing champion base for sector-mapping purposes.
insights.locator: The Netherlands is Europe’s leading hub

insights.claim: Oertel's core policy recommendation is that European policymakers stop treating biomanufacturing as a green niche and instead recognise it as a strategic foundational technology layer for a post-fossil, electrified economy — requiring political leadership as fluent in microbes and enzymes as it already is in rare earths and chips.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: The article's top-line policy ask — a reframing exercise that, if adopted, would likely unlock the infrastructure and capital investment the piece argues is currently missing.
insights.locator: What is holding Europe back is not a lack of capital

insights.claim: Oertel proposes an EU-UK 'grand bargain' pairing European market scale with British scientific strength via joint infrastructure, coordinated funding, university cooperation and shared regulatory sandboxes, alongside a dedicated EU-Japan economic-security track (leveraging Japan's mature but infrastructure-constrained biomanufacturing base) and a new EU-India Trade and Technology Council track building on the recently concluded EU-India Free Trade Agreement.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Lays out three specific coalition-building tracks (UK, Japan, India) that would be concrete near-term diplomatic/investment catalysts to watch for EU biomanufacturing partnerships.
insights.locator: A grand bargain with Britain

insights.claim: The EU's 2025 Bioeconomy Strategy plans to launch a Bio-based Europe Alliance at the Global Bioeconomy Summit in Ireland in September 2026, and the forthcoming Biotech Act II and the proposed European Competitiveness Fund are meant to streamline funding and regulation — but the Industrial Accelerator Act currently mentions bio-based solutions only in passing, an omission Oertel says should be fixed in negotiations with the European Parliament.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Names specific, dated EU legislative vehicles (Biotech Act II, September 2026 summit, Industrial Accelerator Act) that are near-term events where the policy fix Oertel calls for could actually happen.
insights.locator: The 2025 EU Bioeconomy Strategy sets out

insights.claim: Oertel argues Europe's rearmament spending could be a catalyst for biomanufacturing: even a small fraction of Europe's current defence-spending surge redirected to biomanufacturing R&D could generate a large innovation push, and framing industrial renewal as a defence issue could increase support for defence spending in more reluctant EU countries.
insights.speaker: Janka Oertel
insights.so_what: Identifies a political-economy mechanism (linking defence budgets to biomanufacturing) that could unlock funding neither pure climate nor pure industrial-policy framing has secured.
insights.locator: Defence could become an important catalyst
